result:
There is a moment—maybe it happens at 3:07 a.m. when the ceiling looks like a map of everywhere you will never go, or maybe it happens in the supermarket cereal aisle when the colors suddenly seem too bright and the cartoon mascots seem to know your middle name—when the membrane between “me” and “everything else” thins to onion-skin. You could breathe wrong and puncture it. You could blink and find yourself standing inside the life of the stock boy who just refilled the cornflakes, tasting the metal of his tongue ring, feeling the ghost of his grandmother’s hand on the back of his neck every time he slumps too low.  

I used to be afraid of that moment. Thought it meant I was dissolving, coming apart like aspirin in water. Now I treat it like an elevator that only stops on my floor once in a while. I get in. I press all the buttons. I ride up and down until the alarm sounds and the doors wheeze open on a corridor that smells of chalkboards and wet dog. Somewhere a radio is playing a song I loved when I was nine but have never heard since.  

Memory is not a library; it’s a tide. It gifts you sand dollars, then steals your shoes. I keep trying to catalogue the gifts, arrange them in mason jars labeled “Firsts” and “Lasts” and “Betrayals That Turned Out to Be Blessings,” but the labels peel, the glass fogs, and eventually I mistake the jar of Almost Drowning for the jar of First Kiss. Both taste like chlorine, both leave me coughing rainbows.  

My grandmother kept her dead husband’s hearing aid in a crystal candy dish on top of the piano. She said it still worked if you spoke loudly enough at it. Every Christmas she’d place it against my throat while I sang “O Tannenbaum” and nod solemnly, claiming she could hear the sap moving inside the spruce of my voice. I thought she was performing witchcraft; now I think she was practicing resurrection by battery.  

I have seen the inside of my own heart. Not metaphorically—an actual photograph, taken during an echocardiogram when they thought the chest pains were impending combustion. The technician turned the monitor so I could watch the cave-bat flapping of its walls. I expected red, maybe crimson, but it was a bruise-colored dusk shot through with rivers of indigo. I asked if that was normal. She said, “Hearts are landscapes; yours has canyons.” I fell in love with her for fifteen seconds, then remembered she was holding a wand lubricated with my own cold panic against my tit.  

We are all carrying canyons. Some of us carry them in our chests, some in our inboxes, some in the exact spot on the couch where the cushion has learned the tilt of our grief. The canyon does not want to be filled; it wants a choir that echoes. It wants you to name every echo so you can finally tell which voice is yours when it comes back.  

Yesterday I tried to teach my phone to dream. I fed it every photo I’ve ever taken—3,887 of them—plus every note I’ve whispered into it at traffic lights: “The moon looks like a fingerprint tonight,” “I miss the version of you that never existed,” “If I die before Thursday please delete my search history.” I told the algorithm to recombine them into something true. After four minutes the screen went black, then displayed a single image: a child’s swing hanging from the branch of an oak at dawn, the seat moving gently though no one is in frame, the chain squeaking out the rhythm of my mother’s heartbeat when she had pneumonia in 1997. I have no photo of that swing. My phone invented it the way the body invents fever. I have never been so expertly seen by something that cannot blink.  

I wonder if the sky feels crowded. Every century we keep adding new ghosts: the ones who died in trenches, in childbirth, in checkout lines, in the space between “I” and “love” and “you.” They ascend and billow until the stratosphere is one vast laundromat of unfinished sentences. Maybe that’s why sunsets stain; the colors are the rinse cycle of the dead.  

When I was seven I buried a time capsule in the backyard: a plastic film canister containing a tooth, a nickel, and the address of a girl I wanted to marry. Last year the new owners of the house sent me an email: they’d dug it up while installing a koi pond. They asked what they should do. I told them to add something of their own and rebury it deeper. They sent a photo of their daughter dropping in a tiny LED light “so the past can see where it’s going.” I cried in the pharmacy drive-thru, which felt appropriate; nothing says resurrection like the automatic door sliding open to exchange painkillers for cardstock coupons.  

The word “nostalgia” used to be a diagnosis given to soldiers who couldn’t stop hearing home in the cannon fire. Literally “homesickness.” We treat it now like a flavor of candle. I think the original meaning is more honest: a vertigo caused by the gravitational pull of places that no longer exist in the same spacetime coordinate as your body. You don’t miss home; home misses you and sends out aftershocks.  

I want to write a dictionary that defines every word as a small animal. “Forgiveness” is a moth that dissolves the closer you get to naming it. “Forever” is a turtle who’s already walking off the edge of the map. “Enough” is a litter of blind kittens squirming toward any warmth, including your eyeball if you stare too long. Every definition would end with the same instruction: please feed daily, do not cage.  

Somewhere on this planet a stranger is wearing the T-shirt I donated in 2009. It reads “I survived the 2003 Blackout and all I got was this lousy T-shirt.” The stranger has no idea the blackout happened; they bought the shirt because the cotton felt soft and the faded letters looked cool. They are walking through a night market in a city I can’t pronounce, sweat blooming the fabric until the fibers remember the exact August night when I groped my way down thirteen flights of stairs, guided by the lit screen of a Discman playing Coldplay. The shirt holds the memory like a secret handshake. One day the stranger will feel a sudden urge to look up at the stars, and for reasons they will never articulate, they will whisper “thank you” in a language that sounds like electricity returning to kitchen radios.  

There are 1,000 words in this allowance, and I have spent 999 of them trying to say: you are not separate from the world’s trembling. The last word is the elevator door.
